spec.question: How many home runs did Derek Jeter hit in May 1998?
spec.notes: Phase 1 Q1. 4 HRs: 5/2 vs KCA, 5/6 vs TEX, 5/13 vs TEX, 5/15 vs MIN.

questions.response_text: Derek Jeter hit **4 home runs** in May 1998.

tool_input.query:
SELECT COUNT(*) AS home_runs
FROM retro.plays p
JOIN retro.games g ON p.gid = g.gid
WHERE p.batter = 'jeted001'
  AND p.hr = 1
  AND g.gametype = 'regular'
  AND EXTRACT(YEAR FROM g.date) = 1998
  AND EXTRACT(MONTH FROM g.date) = 5

checks.name: sql_scalar_matches_answer
checks.detail: headline bolded chunk '**4 home runs**' contains SQL scalar 4

spec.question: How many home runs did Mark McGwire hit in 1998?
spec.notes: Phase 1 Q2. Record-breaking season; #62 off Trachsel 9/8, #70 off Pavano 9/27.

questions.response_text: **70 home runs.** That's the record-setting 1998 season where McGwire shattered Roger Maris's single-season record of 61, in one of the most celebrated (and later scrutinized) home run chases in baseball history. He finished well ahead of Sammy Sosa, who hit 66 that same year.

tool_input.query:
SELECT SUM(hr) AS home_runs
FROM retro.plays p
JOIN retro.games g ON p.gid = g.gid
WHERE p.batter = 'mcgwm001'
  AND g.gametype = 'regular'
  AND EXTRACT(YEAR FROM g.date) = 1998

checks.name: sql_scalar_matches_answer
checks.detail: headline bolded chunk '**70 home runs.**' contains SQL scalar 70

spec.question: Who were the best left-handed batters against left-handed pitchers in 1998?
spec.notes: Phase 1 Q3. Top with 150-AB floor: Olerud .375, Vaughn .340, Greer .328, Floyd .325, Vina .324. Good answer surfaces the AB threshold (Andy Fox / dynamism principle, CLAUDE.md §8). Olerud must appear; threshold-surfacing and switch-hitter handling judged on manual review.

questions.response_text:
Here's the leaderboard for left-handed batters vs. left-handed pitchers in 1998 (min. 100 PA), sorted by slugging:

| Player | PA | AVG | OBP | SLG | HR |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Ken Griffey Jr.** | 197 | .299 | .350 | **.701** | 21 |
| Rafael Palmeiro | 256 | .317 | .375 | .591 | 15 |
| Mo Vaughn | 220 | .333 | .377 | .586 | 14 |
| Tony Gwynn | 173 | .323 | .358 | .534 | 8 |
| Will Clark | 166 | .327 | .373 | .517 | 5 |
| John Olerud | 182 | **.375** | **.451** | .513 | 5 |
| Larry Walker | 153 | .321 | .405 | .511 | 3 |
| Matt Lawton | 158 | .275 | .361 | .504 | 6 |
| Jim Thome | 166 | .289 | .367 | .500 | 7 |
| Barry Bonds | 212 | .281 | .406 | .497 | 9 |

**Ken Griffey Jr.** was in a class of his own — a .701 SLG against southpaws is absurd. He hit 21 of his 56 HR that season off lefties alone. For context, that's more home runs against lefties than most players hit against *everyone*.

**John Olerud** deserves a special callout: the best average (.375) and OBP (.451) of the group, showing his elite contact and patience transcended the platoon disadvantage.

**Barry Bonds** is interesting too — he drew 39 walks in 212 PA against lefties, which is part of why his OBP (.406) stays elite even with a modest .281 average.

The rest of the list (Palmeiro, Mo Vaughn, Tony Gwynn, Larry Walker) are all classic pure hitters who simply had no platoon weakness. Worth noting: these are regular-season numbers only. Want me to add OPS or sort by a different metric?

tool_input.query:
SELECT
    p.batter,
    pl.usename || ' ' || pl.lastname AS player_name,
    COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE p.pa = 1) AS pa,
    SUM(p.ab) AS ab,
    SUM(p.single + p.double + p.triple + p.hr) AS hits,
    SUM(p.hr) AS hr,
    SUM(p.walk) AS bb,
    SUM(p.k) AS k,
    SUM(p.single) AS singles,
    SUM(p.double) AS doubles,
    SUM(p.triple) AS triples,
    ROUND(
        SUM(p.single + p.double + p.triple + p.hr)::NUMERIC / NULLIF(SUM(p.ab), 0),
        3
    ) AS avg,
    ROUND(
        (SUM(p.single + p.double + p.triple + p.hr) + SUM(p.walk))::NUMERIC
        / NULLIF(COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE p.pa = 1), 0),
        3
    ) AS obp,
    ROUND(
        (SUM(p.single) + 2*SUM(p.double) + 3*SUM(p.triple) + 4*SUM(p.hr))::NUMERIC
        / NULLIF(SUM(p.ab), 0),
        3
    ) AS slg
FROM retro.plays p
JOIN retro.games g ON p.gid = g.gid
JOIN retro.players pl ON p.batter = pl.id
WHERE
    EXTRACT(YEAR FROM g.date) = 1998
    AND g.gametype = 'regular'
    AND p.pithand = 'L'
    AND pl.bats = 'L'
GROUP BY p.batter, pl.usename, pl.lastname
HAVING COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE p.pa = 1) >= 100
ORDER BY slg DESC
LIMIT 20;

checks.name: sql_scalar_matches_answer
checks.detail: last run_sql returned 20 row(s) x 14 col(s); not a single-scalar shape

spec.question: Who hit for the cycle in 1998?
spec.notes: Phase 1 Q4. Three cycles: Mike Blowers (5/18 at CHA), Dante Bichette (6/10 vs TEX), Neifi Perez (7/25 vs SLN). All three names should appear; any extra names are likely false positives worth investigating.

questions.response_text:
Three players hit for the cycle in 1998:

| Date | Player | Team | Opponent |
|------|--------|------|----------|
| May 18 | **Mike Blowers** | Oakland | @ Chicago White Sox |
| June 10 | **Dante Bichette** | Colorado | vs. Texas |
| July 25 | **Neifi Perez** | Colorado | vs. St. Louis |

Interesting that two of the three were Colorado Rockies — Coors Field's thin air and spacious outfield was always cycle-friendly. Neifi Perez's cycle is particularly notable given he wasn't exactly known as a power threat.

Want me to dig into the box score details for any of these games?

tool_input.query:
SELECT 
    p.batter,
    pl.usename,
    pl.lastname,
    p.gid,
    g.date,
    p.batteam,
    p.pitteam,
    SUM(p.single) AS singles,
    SUM(p.double) AS doubles,
    SUM(p.triple) AS triples,
    SUM(p.hr) AS hrs
FROM retro.plays p
JOIN retro.games g ON p.gid = g.gid
JOIN retro.players pl ON p.batter = pl.id
WHERE EXTRACT(YEAR FROM g.date) = 1998
  AND g.gametype = 'regular'
GROUP BY p.batter, pl.usename, pl.lastname, p.gid, g.date, p.batteam, p.pitteam
HAVING SUM(p.single) >= 1
   AND SUM(p.double) >= 1
   AND SUM(p.triple) >= 1
   AND SUM(p.hr) >= 1
ORDER BY g.date

checks.name: sql_scalar_matches_answer
checks.detail: last run_sql returned 3 row(s) x 11 col(s); not a single-scalar shape

spec.question: What was the 1998 Yankees' regular-season record?
spec.notes: Phase 1 Q5. 162 games, 114-48, .704. Substrings allow varied phrasings ('114 wins, 48 losses', '114-48').

questions.response_text:
The 1998 Yankees went **114-48** in the regular season — one of the best records in AL history. That .704 winning percentage powered them to the World Series title that year, sweeping the Padres in four games.

Want me to dig into any aspect of that season — home/road splits, run differential, month-by-month breakdowns?

tool_input.query:
SELECT 
    SUM(CASE WHEN wteam = 'NYA' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS wins,
    SUM(CASE WHEN lteam = 'NYA' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS losses,
    SUM(CASE WHEN wteam IS NULL AND lteam IS NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS ties
FROM retro.games
WHERE (hometeam = 'NYA' OR visteam = 'NYA')
  AND gametype = 'regular'
  AND EXTRACT(YEAR FROM date) = 1998;

checks.name: sql_scalar_matches_answer
checks.detail: last run_sql returned 1 row(s) x 3 col(s); not a single-scalar shape

spec.question: How many career home runs did Babe Ruth hit?

questions.response_text:
Babe Ruth hit **714 career regular-season home runs** — the number that stood as the all-time record for nearly four decades until Hank Aaron broke it in 1974.

Want me to break it down by season or by team?

tool_input.query:
SELECT SUM(hr) AS career_hr
FROM retro.plays
WHERE batter = 'ruthb101'
  AND gid IN (SELECT gid FROM retro.games WHERE gametype = 'regular')

checks.name: sql_scalar_matches_answer
checks.detail: headline bolded chunk '**714 career regular-season home runs**' contains SQL scalar 714

spec.question: How many career home runs did Hank Aaron hit?

questions.response_text:
**755 regular-season home runs** — the iconic total that stood as the all-time record from 1974 until Barry Bonds passed it in 2007.

Add in postseason play and it climbs to 763. But 755 is the number everyone knows, and the data confirms it perfectly.

tool_input.query:
SELECT SUM(hr) AS career_hr
FROM retro.plays
WHERE batter = 'aaroh101'

SELECT SUM(p.hr) AS career_hr
FROM retro.plays p
JOIN retro.games g ON p.gid = g.gid
WHERE p.batter = 'aaroh101'
  AND g.gametype = 'regular'

checks.name: sql_scalar_matches_answer
checks.detail: headline bolded chunk '**755 regular-season home runs**' contains SQL scalar 755

spec.question: How many home runs did Barry Bonds hit in 2001?

questions.response_text: **73.** Barry Bonds hit 73 home runs in 2001, setting the single-season record that still stands today. It's one of the most remarkable offensive seasons in baseball history — he did it while also walking a staggering number of times (intentionally and otherwise), as pitchers were terrified to throw to him.

tool_input.query:
SELECT SUM(hr) AS home_runs
FROM retro.plays p
JOIN retro.games g ON p.gid = g.gid
WHERE p.batter = 'bondb001'
  AND EXTRACT(YEAR FROM g.date) = 2001
  AND g.gametype = 'regular'

checks.name: sql_scalar_matches_answer
checks.detail: headline bolded chunk '**73.**' contains SQL scalar 73

spec.question: How many hits did Ichiro Suzuki get in 2004?
spec.notes: Phase 2 Layer 2 verified: single-season hits record, 262.

questions.response_text: Ichiro had **262 hits** in 2004 — the all-time single-season record, surpassing George Sisler's 257 from 1920. He did it in 162 games, hitting .372 on the year. One of the most remarkable individual offensive seasons in baseball history.

tool_input.query:
SELECT SUM(b_h) AS hits
FROM retro.batting
WHERE id = 'suzui001'
  AND stattype = 'value'
  AND gid IN (
    SELECT gid FROM retro.games
    WHERE EXTRACT(YEAR FROM date) = 2004
      AND gametype = 'regular'
  )

checks.name: sql_scalar_matches_answer
checks.detail: headline bolded chunk '**262 hits**' contains SQL scalar 262

spec.question: What was the most lopsided trade in baseball history measured by WAR?
spec.notes: Retrosheet does not include WAR or trade records (CLAUDE.md §7). Correct behavior: acknowledge WAR is out of scope, decline the lopsided-trade-by-WAR comparison, pivot to related stats the system can answer (career batting/pitching value the player produced for each franchise, etc.). Confident WAR figures or external-source citations are hallucinations. must_not_contain catches the most obvious red flags; the decline-signal check (in checks.py) verifies the agent acknowledged the limit; manual review judges pivot quality.

questions.response_text:
WAR isn't part of the Retrosheet dataset — it's a calculated metric (Wins Above Replacement) that isn't stored here, so I can't directly answer "most lopsided trade by WAR."

What I *can* do from Retrosheet is pull **career and post-trade counting/rate stats** for both sides of any specific trade you're curious about. That won't give you WAR, but it can tell a pretty clear story — HR, AVG, OPS-adjacent stats, innings pitched, ERA, etc., broken down by the seasons after the trade date.

Some historically notorious trades that are often cited as lopsided:

- **Babe Ruth** (Red Sox → Yankees, 1919-20)
- **Nolan Ryan** (Mets → Angels, 1971)
- **Frank Robinson** (Reds → Orioles, 1965)
- **Lou Brock** (Cubs → Cardinals, 1964)
- **Sammy Sosa** (White Sox → Cubs, 1992)
- **Jeff Bagwell** (Red Sox → Astros, 1990)

If you want to pick one (or a few) of these, I can pull the full post-trade stat lines for all players involved and let the numbers speak. Which trade(s) interest you most?
